_readme: Regulatory/political reception layer, hand-built 2026-07 from primary sources. score: 0-100 reception for large BYOP datacenter+PV builds. confidence: high = specific instruments/decisions verified in primary sources this session; medium = documented projects but no verified fast-track instrument; low = posture inferred, no direct evidence. This layer is interpretive by design — the dossier text and citations ARE the data, the score is just its projection onto one axis.

02.name: Aragón
02.instrument: PIGA — Proyecto/Plan de Interés General de Aragón
02.dossier: The most institutionally committed region in Spain. Datacenter projects are declared 'inversión de interés autonómico' then PIGA, unlocking expedited permitting and tax benefits. Microsoft's 'Región MSFT' PIGA (3 campuses: La Muela, Villamayor de Gállego, Zaragoza) initially approved after Sept 2025 submission; AWS expansion PIGA reached its third partial final approval in May 2026, with ~30 datacenter buildings planned. Regional government actively markets land+permits as a package. Nuance: visible local opposition current (water/land use, 'prosperidad destructiva' critique) but so far without decisive political traction.

11.name: Extremadura
11.instrument: PREMIA — Proyecto de Interés Empresarial Autonómico
11.dossier: The emerging second pole, and arguably the better pure-BYOP fit: cheapest land in Spain, top-tier solar, and a government signing protocols at president level. PREMIA status declared for Merlin Edged's Navalmoral de la Mata campus (€1.6B); twin Valdecaballeros (Badajoz) campus announced — each up to 1 GW IT, PUE 1.15, explicitly renewables-driven. Region claims >€6B datacenter investment captured in about a year (Feb 2026). Nuance: thinner fiber/labor base than Madrid/Zaragoza corridors; Almaraz nuclear closure politics cuts both ways (grid headroom vs. anti-infrastructure sentiment).

08.name: Castilla-La Mancha
08.instrument: Project-level support (Meta Talavera); no dedicated fast-track found
08.dossier: Regional government supportive — Meta's Talavera de la Reina hyperscale campus cleared a key step Dec 2025 and construction has begun. But this is also the national lightning rod for datacenter water opposition: peak demand of 120 L/s equals over half of Talavera's consumption, and the project became the reference case for Ecologistas en Acción's national campaign. A BYOP dry-cooled design partially sidesteps the water objection, but the social terrain is pre-heated. Land and solar are excellent.

13.name: Comunidad de Madrid
13.dossier: Spain's colo capital and fiber hub, but the wrong fit for BYOP at scale: the most saturated distribution nodes in the country, expensive fragmented land, and the existing cluster competes for every buildable plot. For an edge/latency-sensitive site the calculus flips — proximity to the Madrid interconnection ecosystem is the point.

07.name: Castilla y León
07.dossier: Land-rich, solar-decent, depopulating provinces actively courting industrial investment; historically fast comparative permitting for utility PV. No flagship datacenter fast-track surfaced in this research pass — treat the score as posture, not proof. Watch item: it borders both the Madrid fiber ring and Aragon corridor.

01.name: Andalucía
01.instrument: 'Unidad Aceleradora de Proyectos' (general investment accelerator, not DC-specific)
01.dossier: Pro-investment administration with a general project-acceleration unit and Spain's largest solar resource. Datacenter activity concentrated around Málaga/Sevilla remains modest. Southern water stress is real and politically salient — dry-cooled BYOP is the defensible configuration here.

10.name: Comunitat Valenciana
10.dossier: Documented hyperscale-class project at Picassent. Coastal land competition and agriculture politics limit cheap large parcels; interior (Requena plateau) is the plausible BYOP zone.

12.name: Galicia
12.dossier: A Coruña datacenter project documented; region is wind-heavy rather than solar-heavy, and the solar case is the weakest on the peninsula — BYOP solar+BESS math is structurally worse here.

16.name: País Vasco / Euskadi
16.dossier: Merlin's Arasur (Ribera Baja, Álava) site documented. Strong industrial fabric and fiber, but scarce cheap land and modest solar resource cap the BYOP case.

09.name: Cataluña
09.dossier: Active colo market in Barcelona, but for greenfield BYOP: severely saturated grid nodes, dense land use, comparatively slow renewables permitting (Catalonia has chronically lagged Spain in utility PV build-out), and strong organized environmental activism. No fast-track instrument surfaced.

14.name: Región de Murcia
14.dossier: Top-3 solar resource and cheap interior land; acute water stress makes any wet-cooled design politically radioactive — dry-cooled BYOP only. No DC-specific instrument found.

15.name: Navarra
15.dossier: Renewables-experienced administration; modest land availability and solar vs. the southern meseta. No datacenter signal found either way.

17.name: La Rioja
17.dossier: Small region, no signal found either way.

03.name: Principado de Asturias
03.dossier: Industrial land and cool climate but weak solar; BYOP solar math is poor. No signal found.

06.name: Cantabria
06.dossier: Weak solar, mountainous, no signal found.

04.name: Illes Balears
04.dossier: Island grid, extreme land scarcity, tourism-driven land politics hostile to industrial use. Included for completeness; interesting only for small edge sites serving local demand.
